IMPACT.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The paper tackles a useful applied problem for Vietnamese NLP by building a new readability corpus and proposing a lightweight formula that attains ~81% overall accuracy on their data; this resource-centric contribution can be practically useful. However, the impact is limited by methodological gaps (no comparison to baselines or ML models, shallow surface features only, and no code/hyperparameter disclosure) and by indications that some external frequency lists poorly correlate with readability. Given these constraints, the work is a moderate contribution that may enable follow-up studies and resource reuse but is unlikely to change broader research practice without stronger evaluation and reproducibility. | B(3): Building a sizable Vietnamese readability corpus and a simple, high-accuracy formula addresses a clear practical gap for a low-resource language and provides a useful baseline for educators and NLP practitioners. However, the reliance on surface features, absence of comparisons to baselines or machine-learning methods, and limited analysis of generalizability constrain how much this will change current research practices. The work is likely to be used as a starting resource and to stimulate follow-up studies (richer features, better difficulty lists, cross-corpus validation), but it is not transformative for the broader readability field.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper tackles a useful applied problem for Vietnamese NLP by building a new readability corpus and proposing a lightweight formula that attains ~81% overall accuracy on their data; this resource-centric contribution can be practically useful. However, the impact is limited by methodological gaps (no comparison to baselines or ML models, shallow surface features only, and no code/hyperparameter disclosure) and by indications that some external frequency lists poorly correlate with readability. Given these constraints, the work is a moderate contribution that may enable follow-up studies and resource reuse but is unlikely to change broader research practice without stronger evaluation and reproducibility.

reviewer_b.justification: Building a sizable Vietnamese readability corpus and a simple, high-accuracy formula addresses a clear practical gap for a low-resource language and provides a useful baseline for educators and NLP practitioners. However, the reliance on surface features, absence of comparisons to baselines or machine-learning methods, and limited analysis of generalizability constrain how much this will change current research practices. The work is likely to be used as a starting resource and to stimulate follow-up studies (richer features, better difficulty lists, cross-corpus validation), but it is not transformative for the broader readability field.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The topic (Vietnamese text readability) is relevant to top-tier NLP venues, but the submission as summarized lacks key elements expected by that community: no comparisons to baselines, limited feature set and evaluation (accuracy/Fleiss' kappa only), and missing reproducibility details (code, dataset annotation protocol, inter-annotator agreement beyond a single kappa mention). The corpus construction and claim of a new formula could be of interest, but without rigorous annotation methodology, statistical significance or ML baselines and ablation studies the work reads as an applied/engineering contribution better suited to a specialized or regional venue unless expanded with stronger evaluation and reproducibility materials. | B(3): The paper addresses an NLP problem (readability assessment) and contributes a new Vietnamese corpus, which is relevant to a top-tier venue. However, the methodological scope is limited (surface features and a single formula), there are no baselines or comparative ML approaches, and evaluation is confined to the built corpus—weakening its appeal to the broader NLP/ML community. With stronger evaluation (comparisons, code, broader features or modeling) it would better fit a top conference; as is, it is borderline appropriate.

reviewer_a.justification: The topic (Vietnamese text readability) is relevant to top-tier NLP venues, but the submission as summarized lacks key elements expected by that community: no comparisons to baselines, limited feature set and evaluation (accuracy/Fleiss' kappa only), and missing reproducibility details (code, dataset annotation protocol, inter-annotator agreement beyond a single kappa mention). The corpus construction and claim of a new formula could be of interest, but without rigorous annotation methodology, statistical significance or ML baselines and ablation studies the work reads as an applied/engineering contribution better suited to a specialized or regional venue unless expanded with stronger evaluation and reproducibility materials.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper addresses an NLP problem (readability assessment) and contributes a new Vietnamese corpus, which is relevant to a top-tier venue. However, the methodological scope is limited (surface features and a single formula), there are no baselines or comparative ML approaches, and evaluation is confined to the built corpus—weakening its appeal to the broader NLP/ML community. With stronger evaluation (comparisons, code, broader features or modeling) it would better fit a top conference; as is, it is borderline appropriate.

ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The paper contributes a new large Vietnamese readability corpus, which is a useful resource and a genuine data contribution beyond prior Vietnamese studies (e.g., work on text-length factors). However, the proposed formula relies on conventional surface features (average sentence/word length, percent difficult words/syllables) rather than introducing fundamentally new methods, and no baselines or comparisons to existing readability models are reported to demonstrate a substantive methodological advance. Thus the work is an incremental but practical contribution (notable dataset plus an empirical formula) rather than a highly novel methodological breakthrough. | B(3): From an applied-linguistics perspective the paper makes a useful but essentially incremental contribution: the construction of a new large Vietnamese readability corpus is a meaningful and novel resource for the community, but the proposed formula relies on traditional surface features (sentence/word length, % difficult words/syllables) already used in prior Vietnamese work (e.g. 2017 study on text-length factors). The absence of comparisons to baselines or more linguistically informed features limits the methodological novelty, so the work is more of a practical resource plus modestly novel empirical validation than a fundamentally new method that would surprise domain experts.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper contributes a new large Vietnamese readability corpus, which is a useful resource and a genuine data contribution beyond prior Vietnamese studies (e.g., work on text-length factors). However, the proposed formula relies on conventional surface features (average sentence/word length, percent difficult words/syllables) rather than introducing fundamentally new methods, and no baselines or comparisons to existing readability models are reported to demonstrate a substantive methodological advance. Thus the work is an incremental but practical contribution (notable dataset plus an empirical formula) rather than a highly novel methodological breakthrough.

reviewer_b.justification: From an applied-linguistics perspective the paper makes a useful but essentially incremental contribution: the construction of a new large Vietnamese readability corpus is a meaningful and novel resource for the community, but the proposed formula relies on traditional surface features (sentence/word length, % difficult words/syllables) already used in prior Vietnamese work (e.g. 2017 study on text-length factors). The absence of comparisons to baselines or more linguistically informed features limits the methodological novelty, so the work is more of a practical resource plus modestly novel empirical validation than a fundamentally new method that would surprise domain experts.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: [Consensus avg=1] A(1): The paper does not compare against any appropriate baselines (no prior models or simple baselines such as traditional readability formulas or recent Vietnamese-specific methods like the 2017 study on Vietnamese readability), so it fails to situate its performance claims. Without baseline comparisons or standardized test splits and metrics reported for competitors, fairness of evaluation cannot be assessed and claims of superior accuracy are unsupported. The authors note related work but do not empirically acknowledge when prior methods perform comparably or better, nor provide analyses contrasting their formula with existing approaches. From a reproducibility and methodological standpoint this is insufficient: key comparative baselines and controlled experiments are missing. | B(1): The paper reports no comparisons against prior or standard baselines (summary states “Baselines compared: none mentioned”), so it fails to situate the new formula relative to existing Vietnamese readability work (e.g., studies addressing text-length and readability in Vietnamese). Without experiments on shared datasets or against established methods, there is no fair head-to-head evaluation and no analysis showing when prior approaches perform comparably or better. While the related work coverage is described as adequate, absence of empirical baselines makes the comparison component of the contribution inadequate.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper does not compare against any appropriate baselines (no prior models or simple baselines such as traditional readability formulas or recent Vietnamese-specific methods like the 2017 study on Vietnamese readability), so it fails to situate its performance claims. Without baseline comparisons or standardized test splits and metrics reported for competitors, fairness of evaluation cannot be assessed and claims of superior accuracy are unsupported. The authors note related work but do not empirically acknowledge when prior methods perform comparably or better, nor provide analyses contrasting their formula with existing approaches. From a reproducibility and methodological standpoint this is insufficient: key comparative baselines and controlled experiments are missing.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper reports no comparisons against prior or standard baselines (summary states “Baselines compared: none mentioned”), so it fails to situate the new formula relative to existing Vietnamese readability work (e.g., studies addressing text-length and readability in Vietnamese). Without experiments on shared datasets or against established methods, there is no fair head-to-head evaluation and no analysis showing when prior approaches perform comparably or better. While the related work coverage is described as adequate, absence of empirical baselines makes the comparison component of the contribution inadequate.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The paper states clear empirical claims and presents an evaluation on a newly built Vietnamese corpus, but the summary reveals important methodological gaps that weaken those claims. Key reproducibility details are missing (corpus construction/annotation protocol, splits, code/hyperparameters), no baseline models or ablation studies are reported, and there is no mention of statistical significance or confidence intervals around the reported accuracies. Given these omissions and the admitted limitation that some frequent-word lists poorly correlate with readability, the conclusions are plausible but not yet fully supported by rigorous experimental evidence. | B(3): The paper states clear claims (new Vietnamese readability corpus and a surface-feature formula with reported ~81% overall accuracy) and provides empirical evaluation on the constructed corpus, but the experimental methodology is weak: no baselines or comparative models, no reported statistical tests, and limited feature validation. There are no mathematical derivations to check, and several unsupported assumptions remain (e.g., using Dien and Hao frequency lists as proxies for ‘easy’ items despite finding low correlation). Given these issues, the conclusions are partly supported by the reported results on the single corpus but lack sufficient controls and comparative evidence to fully validate generality.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper states clear empirical claims and presents an evaluation on a newly built Vietnamese corpus, but the summary reveals important methodological gaps that weaken those claims. Key reproducibility details are missing (corpus construction/annotation protocol, splits, code/hyperparameters), no baseline models or ablation studies are reported, and there is no mention of statistical significance or confidence intervals around the reported accuracies. Given these omissions and the admitted limitation that some frequent-word lists poorly correlate with readability, the conclusions are plausible but not yet fully supported by rigorous experimental evidence.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper states clear claims (new Vietnamese readability corpus and a surface-feature formula with reported ~81% overall accuracy) and provides empirical evaluation on the constructed corpus, but the experimental methodology is weak: no baselines or comparative models, no reported statistical tests, and limited feature validation. There are no mathematical derivations to check, and several unsupported assumptions remain (e.g., using Dien and Hao frequency lists as proxies for ‘easy’ items despite finding low correlation). Given these issues, the conclusions are partly supported by the reported results on the single corpus but lack sufficient controls and comparative evidence to fully validate generality.

CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): Overall the paper appears well organized with a clear progression from corpus construction to feature extraction, formula derivation, and evaluation, and the main contributions (new corpus and a readability formula) are stated early. Notation for the surface features is described and seems consistent, and the writing quality is reported as adequate. However, the summary does not indicate whether figures, tables, or examples effectively illustrate results, and some presentation gaps remain (e.g., no code/hyperparameter details reported), so minor clarity and reproducibility-related presentation improvements are needed. | B(4): Overall the paper is well organized and follows a logical flow from corpus creation through feature extraction to evaluation, and the main contributions (new Vietnamese corpus and a surface-feature readability formula) are clearly stated early. Notation and methodological descriptions appear consistent and readable from the summary, and writing quality is described as adequate with no major grammatical issues. However, presentation could be improved by clearer reporting of figures/tables and examples (not mentioned in the summary) and by more explicit definition of some feature computations to aid reproducibility.

reviewer_a.justification: Overall the paper appears well organized with a clear progression from corpus construction to feature extraction, formula derivation, and evaluation, and the main contributions (new corpus and a readability formula) are stated early. Notation for the surface features is described and seems consistent, and the writing quality is reported as adequate. However, the summary does not indicate whether figures, tables, or examples effectively illustrate results, and some presentation gaps remain (e.g., no code/hyperparameter details reported), so minor clarity and reproducibility-related presentation improvements are needed.

reviewer_b.justification: Overall the paper is well organized and follows a logical flow from corpus creation through feature extraction to evaluation, and the main contributions (new Vietnamese corpus and a surface-feature readability formula) are clearly stated early. Notation and methodological descriptions appear consistent and readable from the summary, and writing quality is described as adequate with no major grammatical issues. However, presentation could be improved by clearer reporting of figures/tables and examples (not mentioned in the summary) and by more explicit definition of some feature computations to aid reproducibility.

SUBSTANCE.justification: [Consensus avg=2] A(2): The experimental evidence is shallow: the paper builds a new corpus and proposes a formula but reports only accuracy and Fleiss' kappa without comparing to any baselines (classic formulas or ML classifiers) or providing ablation/sensitivity analyses of the proposed features. Reproducibility is weak — corpus construction, annotation protocol/inter-annotator agreement details, train/test splits, and code/hyperparameters are not provided — and only limited failure/limitation discussion is present. While related work coverage is described as adequate, the lack of baseline comparisons and robustness checks (e.g., cross-validation, statistical significance, alternative datasets) leaves the claims under-supported. | B(3): The paper provides a substantial empirical contribution (a new Vietnamese corpus) and reports class-wise accuracies and agreement (Fleiss' kappa), which supports the core claim that a surface-feature formula can reach ~81% overall accuracy. However, experiments lack comparisons to baselines or prior Vietnamese work (e.g., studies on text-length effects), and there are no ablation or sensitivity analyses to show which features drive performance. Reproducibility is weak—code and hyperparameters are not mentioned—and while some limitations are acknowledged (poor fit of existing frequent-word lists), deeper failure-mode analysis and broader feature explorations are missing. Given these gaps, the experimental evaluation is adequate but omits several important analyses needed for stronger validation.

reviewer_a.justification: The experimental evidence is shallow: the paper builds a new corpus and proposes a formula but reports only accuracy and Fleiss' kappa without comparing to any baselines (classic formulas or ML classifiers) or providing ablation/sensitivity analyses of the proposed features. Reproducibility is weak — corpus construction, annotation protocol/inter-annotator agreement details, train/test splits, and code/hyperparameters are not provided — and only limited failure/limitation discussion is present. While related work coverage is described as adequate, the lack of baseline comparisons and robustness checks (e.g., cross-validation, statistical significance, alternative datasets) leaves the claims under-supported.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper provides a substantial empirical contribution (a new Vietnamese corpus) and reports class-wise accuracies and agreement (Fleiss' kappa), which supports the core claim that a surface-feature formula can reach ~81% overall accuracy. However, experiments lack comparisons to baselines or prior Vietnamese work (e.g., studies on text-length effects), and there are no ablation or sensitivity analyses to show which features drive performance. Reproducibility is weak—code and hyperparameters are not mentioned—and while some limitations are acknowledged (poor fit of existing frequent-word lists), deeper failure-mode analysis and broader feature explorations are missing. Given these gaps, the experimental evaluation is adequate but omits several important analyses needed for stronger validation.

main_claims: Created a new large corpus for Vietnamese text readability assessment.
Proposed a new formula for predicting Vietnamese text readability using features such as sentence/word length and percentage of difficult words/syllables.
The proposed formula predicts >76% of easy texts, >85% of normal texts, >78% of difficult texts and achieves overall accuracy up to ~81%.
_summary.methodology: Constructed a corpus, extracted surface features (average sentence length in words/syllables/characters, average word length in syllables/characters, percentage of difficult words/syllables using two 1,000-item frequent lists), and built a readability formula evaluated on the corpus.
datasets: easy documents subset of the built corpus
Dien and Hao frequent word/syllable lists (Dinh and Hao, 2015)
limitations: Percentage of difficult syllables (PDS1) and words (PDW1) computed from Dien and Hao's lists are lowly correlated with readability on the built corpus, indicating those lists are poor replacements for easy-word/syllable lists.
Current formula could be improved by using other corpora with more detailed difficulty levels, deeper features (POS, sentence structure, discourse), and machine learning classifiers.

reviewer_a.name: Methodological Experimentalist
reviewer_a.background: Senior ML engineer and researcher with extensive experience in NLP evaluation, dataset creation, and reproducible empirical studies; has reviewed and built many benchmark tasks and reproducible toolchains.
traits: Insists on clear dataset construction procedures (sampling, annotation protocol, inter-annotator agreement) and exposes potential biases
Requires strong baselines, ablation studies, and error analyses to support claims about model/formula improvements
Looks for appropriate statistical testing (confidence intervals, significance tests) and robustness checks (cross-validation, held-out sets)
Values reproducibility: requests code, data splits, feature extraction details, and runtime/implementation specifics
reviewer_a.reviewing_style: Systematically evaluates experimental design and empirical claims, prioritizing rigorous evidence, transparency, and repeatability before accepting strong performance statements.

reviewer_b.name: Applied Linguistics & Readability Specialist
reviewer_b.background: Senior researcher in computational linguistics and readability assessment with domain expertise in low-resource languages and education applications; has published work on readability metrics and their pedagogical/clinical utility.
traits: Evaluates linguistic validity and interpretability of features and whether they align with human notions of readability
Assesses novelty relative to prior readability formulas and corpora, and whether advances meaningfully improve downstream use (education, assessment, NLP preprocessing)
Pays attention to corpus representativeness across genres, registers, and demographics and to potential societal impacts of misclassification
Considers applicability beyond the evaluated corpus (generalizability) and whether the proposed formula is usable by practitioners
reviewer_b.reviewing_style: Focuses on the contribution's novelty, linguistic soundness, and practical impact, weighing whether the work advances understanding or utility in real-world readability applications.
